text: This is a case of a patient who had a colonoscopy and found a mass at thirty five centimeters biopsy proven adenocarcinoma. The area was not tattooed. It was described as being between two and three centimeters. The patient got on the OR schedule without having repeat colonoscopy and biopsy.

interestingly despite the appearance of this colon the patient has never had an attack of diverticulitis before the only surgeries that the patient recently had was bilateral inguinal and an umbilical hernia repair all done simultaneously a couple months earlier i didn't see any evidence externally of a cancer at the outset of the operation

The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the colon, aiming to improve visualization of the entire colon for potential external features of cancer. A colonoscope is brought into the room for an on-table colonoscopy to locate the lesion.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon, revealing severe diverticulosis throughout the bowel. The visual inspection shows the disease extending up to the splenic flexure, indicating the need for a full left hemicolectomy.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative colonoscopy, navigating through the colon with visible diverticula and large stool balls intermittently obstructing the lumen. The colonoscope is advanced up to the splenic flexure, approximately seventy centimeters from the anal verge, with careful withdrawal and camera adjustments to provide an endoscopic view of the colon's interior.

But I didn't see anything that looked like a cancer, which was quite frustrating. Um, for myself and then the patient after I woke them up, uh, because I decided not to move forward with a blind resection. Um, I think we all have been fooled before and because I had not done anything that was a point of no return. My plan was to send this patient back to his endoscopist for a colonoscopy and a tattooing of the lesion.
This was the first time that I was not able to identify a lesion myself, which was quite defeating, but I feel like this was the safest option for the patient. Interested in your thoughts.
